title: Seven-Point Story Structure
author: Dan Wells
source: Dan Wells lecture series, c. 2011. Explicitly derived from Lester Dent's pulp-fiction master formula.

lineage.note: Hook, Plot Turn 1, Pinch 1, Midpoint, Pinch 2, Plot Turn 2, Resolution. Distinctive method: plot BACKWARD from the resolution so the hook is the mirror-image of the ending.

stages.name: Hook
stages.function: Starting state — chosen to mirror or contrast the Resolution. Where the protagonist is at the beginning of their arc.

stages.name: Plot Turn 1
stages.function: Disruption; the story is set in motion. End of Act I.

stages.name: Pinch 1
stages.function: First pressure point — antagonist applies force; protagonist must commit further.

stages.name: Midpoint
stages.function: Protagonist shifts from reactive to proactive. The pivot.

stages.name: Pinch 2
stages.function: Second pressure point — much harder than Pinch 1. The wheels start coming off.

stages.name: Plot Turn 2
stages.function: Protagonist obtains the final piece needed to solve the central problem. End of Act II.

stages.name: Resolution
stages.function: Final state — mirror or contrast to Hook. The arc completes.

applicability.limitations: Backwards-planning requires knowing the ending — bad fit for documentary where the ending is discovered. But the Hook/Resolution mirror is still useful retrospectively in assembly.
